items.title: Know Your Customer (KYC) overview
items.description: Know Your Customer (KYC) is the broad practice of establishing and maintaining an understanding of who a customer is and the nature of the relationship. It usually combines identity verification, collection of background information, and assessment of risk before and during a relationship. The purpose is to ensure the institution can make informed decisions and meet its obligations. In software, KYC is best modelled as a record of evidence and decisions with a defined refresh cycle rather than a one-off checkbox.
items.applicability: Applies when designing onboarding and ongoing-relationship modules in banking and management-company middleware that must capture and maintain customer knowledge.
items.example: Before activation, a customer record holds verified identity evidence, declared purpose of the relationship, and an assigned risk rating, each timestamped and attributable.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal, regulatory, or compliance advice. KYC obligations are jurisdiction-specific; capturing data is not a legal determination of acceptability. Confirm against current applicable laws and approved compliance procedures.

items.title: Customer Due Diligence (CDD)
items.description: Customer Due Diligence (CDD) is the standard set of checks performed to identify and verify a customer and understand the intended relationship. It commonly includes verifying identity, identifying beneficial owners for entities, and forming a view of expected activity. CDD outputs feed the customer risk rating and the decision to proceed. Systems should retain the evidence relied upon and the resulting decision so the basis can be reviewed later.
items.applicability: Applies to standard-risk onboarding and periodic review in banking and management-company systems.
items.example: For a company, CDD verifies the legal entity, identifies individuals owning above a defined threshold, and records expected transaction patterns used later for monitoring.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. CDD requirements are jurisdiction-specific; completing checks is not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD)
items.description: Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) is a deeper level of investigation applied when a relationship presents higher risk, for example due to the customer type, geography, or product. It typically gathers additional information on source of funds or wealth, ownership, and purpose, and often requires senior sign-off. EDD is proportionate: the higher the assessed risk, the more scrutiny is applied. Software should let the risk rating drive whether EDD steps become mandatory and capture the extra evidence and approval.
items.applicability: Applies to higher-risk onboarding and reviews in banking and management-company environments where standard CDD is insufficient.
items.example: A customer rated high-risk triggers EDD requiring source-of-wealth evidence and approval by a senior compliance officer before activation.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. EDD triggers and steps are jurisdiction-specific; performing EDD is not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Identity verification
items.description: Identity verification confirms that a customer is who they claim to be, typically by checking identity attributes against reliable, independent evidence. Approaches range from documentary checks to electronic verification against trusted data sources, often combined for assurance. The outcome is a recorded verification result, the method used, and any residual gaps. Systems should store the verification basis and result rather than just a pass flag, so the strength of verification can be assessed later.
items.applicability: Applies to onboarding and re-verification steps in banking and management-company middleware.
items.example: An individual's identity is confirmed by matching a government document plus an electronic data check, and the record stores both methods and the combined outcome.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. Acceptable verification methods are jurisdiction-specific; a pass result is not a legal determination of acceptability. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Document collection
items.description: Document collection gathers and retains the evidence supporting identity, authority, ownership, and source of funds. Good practice records the document type, validity dates, who collected it, and where the original or certified copy is held. Documents may expire, so systems should track validity and prompt for refresh rather than treating collection as permanent. Storing structured metadata alongside the file makes later review and evidence assembly straightforward.
items.applicability: Applies to onboarding, mandate setup, and periodic review where supporting documents must be captured and retained.
items.example: A passport is stored with its expiry date and collector, and the system flags it for refresh before the relationship's next periodic review if it will have expired.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. Required documents and retention rules are jurisdiction-specific; collection is not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Customer risk rating
items.description: A customer risk rating summarises the assessed risk of a relationship into a band such as low, medium, or high, derived from factors like customer type, geography, products, and behaviour. The rating drives the depth of due diligence, monitoring intensity, and review frequency. Ratings are reassessed when material information changes. In software, the rating should be a computed, explainable value with the contributing factors retained, not an opaque label, so its basis can be reviewed and challenged.
items.applicability: Applies to risk-based onboarding, monitoring, and review processes in banking and management-company middleware.
items.example: A customer in a higher-risk category with cross-border activity is rated high, which automatically raises monitoring sensitivity and shortens the review interval.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. Risk factors and bands are jurisdiction- and institution-specific; a rating is an internal assessment, not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Ongoing monitoring and periodic review
items.description: Ongoing monitoring keeps customer knowledge current after onboarding, combining continuous transaction monitoring with scheduled periodic reviews of the relationship. Review frequency is usually risk-based, with higher-risk customers reviewed more often. Reviews refresh due-diligence information, reassess the risk rating, and confirm activity remains consistent with expectations. Systems should schedule reviews from the risk rating and record each review's outcome to evidence that the relationship was kept under review.
items.applicability: Applies to lifecycle management of existing relationships in banking and management-company systems.
items.example: A high-risk customer is scheduled for review every twelve months; the review updates documents, reconfirms the rating, and records that activity matched the expected profile.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. Review cadence and triggers are jurisdiction-specific; completing a review is not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Name-matching and false-positive handling
items.description: Name matching compares a subject against reference lists or records using fuzzy techniques that tolerate spelling, transliteration, and ordering differences. Because fuzzy matching is imprecise, it generates false positives that must be reviewed and dispositioned rather than acted on automatically. Effective handling records the match score, the reviewer's decision, and the rationale, and can remember confirmed non-matches to reduce repeat alerts. The aim is to balance not missing true matches against overwhelming reviewers with noise.
items.applicability: Applies to screening and matching components across KYC and transaction screening in banking middleware.
items.example: A close-but-not-exact name match is raised for review; an analyst confirms it is a different person, records the rationale, and the system suppresses the same benign match next time.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. A match or non-match decision is an operational review outcome, not a legal determination. Matching thresholds are jurisdiction- and institution-specific; confirm against approved procedures.
